content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Watched 'Decision to Leave' two weeks ago and it’s still in my head

**Score:** 38
**Body:** Park Chan-wook’s 2022 film. On paper it's a detective story. A man falls off a mountain, the detective investigates, the widow is the obvious suspect. He starts watching her apartment from across the street.

That’s the cover. It is not the movie.

I won’t say much more, because half of why this film stays with you is figuring out what it’s actually doing. I’ll just say I keep noticing mountains and oceans differently since I watched it, and anyone who’s seen it will know exactly what I mean.

Two weeks in and I’m still pulling threads from it. 

Looking for recommendations with the same feel: slow, layered, the kind that keeps unfolding weeks later. Doesn’t have to be Korean or noir.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The classic, Laura, 1944, has an obsessed detective.
  - Haven’t seen Laura, that’s a real gap. Detective falling for a portrait before he meets the woman is basically what Hae-jun is doing through binoculars 80 years later. Fixing that this week.
    - Laura is a phenomenal movie.
2. Burning

Memories of Murder
3. God I love this film, that noir / femme fatale vibe but with a Korean twist. For recs, I'd go old school and Double Indemnity
4. Bring Them Down (2024)

The Seed of the Sacred Fig (2024)

The Girl With the Needle (2024)

Green Border (2023)

Monster (2023)

The Beasts (2022)

Great Freedom (2021)

Beginning (2020)

There is No Evil (2020)

This Is Not a Burial, It's a Resurrection (2019)
  - Genuinely great list, thank you. Monster has been on my watchlist forever, starting there. Beginning and This Is Not a Burial I don’t know at all but the titles alone are doing work. Will work through these.
5. It’s kinda striking how similar Decision to Leave is to Basic Instinct and Sea of Love, two erotic thrillers from the classical period. They are anything but slow, but definitely layered. 

Also check out No Other Choice, Lust Caution, The Handmaiden, Vertigo, In the Cut, The Conversation, and Rear Window. 
  - Solid pulls. Already done Handmaiden (still my favorite of his), Vertigo (the obvious ancestor, every surveillance scene in this film has its fingerprints), Rear Window, and The Conversation. Hadn’t connected it to Basic Instinct until you said it but you’re right, just done in stillness instead of heat. Adding Sea of Love, In the Cut, Lust Caution, and No Other Choice to the list.

## 2. Finally watched The Green Knight (2021)

**Score:** 36
**Body:** Way late to the party on this one, but if you like dark fantasy, move David Lowery’s "The Green Knight" to the top of your list. The visuals shot on location in Ireland are jaw-dropping, and Dev Patel is incredible as a more vulnerable version of your typical knightly hero. It keeps the Arthurian lore so subtle that it feels like a moody, surreal arthouse film rather than a typical fantasy blockbuster.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love this movie so much. I wish it was a way bigger deal so there could be many more movies like this made. Dev Patel is so beautifully subtle and fascinating as Gawain -- his face does so much with so little dialogue! All of the performances are lovely, just a pleasure to watch all the way around. 
2. if you're chasing that same slow-dread arthouse fantasy thing, Valhalla Rising (2009, Nicolas Winding Refn) is essential — mute Norse warrior escaping slavery with a kid, they get on a boat with Christians heading to the Holy Land and end up somewhere way worse, almost no dialogue, just mud and blood and these long stretches of walking through fog. formally it's closer to Tarkovsky than Lord of the Rings. The Witch (2015, Eggers' first one) you've probably hit but sits right next to Green Knight in how it uses folklore, 1630s New England family unraveling in the woods, that goat. Tale of Tales (2015, Matteo Garrone) adapts three 17th-century Italian fairy tales into this extremely dark triptych — queen eating a sea monster's heart to get pregnant, king marrying a flea, that kind of thing — visually gorgeous and genuinely unsettling, no Hollywood clean-up. and Hagazussa (2017, Austrian folk horror about a goatherd accused of witchcraft in the Alps, moves like a fever dream, barely any dialogue, just mountains and isolation and dread building for 90 minutes, extremely patient but if Green Knight worked for you this will too
  - You just going around in film subs and using AI to give recommendations? 


Or are you not an actual person and just AI? 
3. The scene that stuck with me most was >!when he flinched away from that giant!<. But fuck if that ending wasn't an expression of next level logic and poetic beauty
4. Saw this in an almost-empty tiny arthouse theater right around when it released.  The kind of theater that you can get a little bottle of prosecco to bring to your seat and that puts nutritional yeast on the popcorn.  Perfect moviegoing experience.
5. I just wish they had a cool battle scene in it.  They show some siege camp that looked cool af but then went to something else
6. I like to watch movies
7. One of the tightest written movies I have ever seen. 

## 3. Please suggest enjoyable or engrossing movies to watch with son. No sex scenes. No really stupid movies.

**Score:** 37
**Body:** Looking for an enjoyable and engrossing movie to watch with my 15 year old without sex scenes or being overly raunchy.  We watch a TON of movies, that’s why it’s difficult to think of one.  He really liked Snack Shack, Almost Famous and The Kings of Summer.  (He likes lots of movies but these “coming of age” films were really enjoyable to him.). I think Super Bad is too raunchy to watch yet.  
Any ideas?  No horror or animation please.  
—-  
Wow, checked in on this and you guys are awesome.  Looking forward to checking out all the suggestions before bed!!  Thanks so much!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Big Trouble in Little China 
2. The Truman Show
3. Jo Jo rabbit 

Its a taika waititi movie.  It might be a little outside the box,  you can watch a trailer on YouTube.  
4. Lord of the Rings trilogy
5. Please watch Sing Street (2016) with your son.  It's a coming-of-age film about a group of boys, who are your son's age, that start a band to impress a girl.  It's an Irish film that takes place in Dublin in the 1980s.  Some of the accents are strong so use cc.

If you like 80s pop music (who doesn't), you'll love the soundtrack.  The original songs are great also.

A little bit of swearing, a little bit of smoking, a little bit of bullying, but nothing too strong for a 15 yr old.

I'm not exaggerating when I say this is the best movie I've watched this millennium.  I saw it for the first time about 2 years ago, and have watched it at least 15 times since.  PLEASE SHOW THUS MOVIE TO YOUR SON.
6. The way way back
7. I always recommend Hunt for the Wilderpeople. 
8. Smoke Signals is a wonderful film that deals with father / son issues in a Native American context.

Bend it like Beckham is a fun coming of age soccer ⚽️ movie told from an immigrant girls perspective.  

I always recommend Tender Mercies.  It’s a wonderful, quiet redemption story.  Robert Duvall won an Oscar for his portrayal of the lead character.
9. Twister/Twisters

The first Ghostbusters and the newest 2 sequels that have the kids

The Star Wars movies (prequels, backstories and the OG)

Time Bandits (an older movie but really fun)
10. *The Peanut Butter Falcon*

*Back to the Future* trilogy

## 4. Movies like Civil War (2024)

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Last year I watched A24’s film Civil War directed by Alex Garland and It’s one of my favorite dystopian movies about a modern civil war in America now ever since I’ve seen Civil War multiple times I wanna watch movies that have a very similar story and plot to Civil War but in a different way now If anyone has suggestions for me please let me know because I really want to checkout movies or shows that are like or similar to Civil War. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Children of Men
2. If you like Alex Garland, watch his show Devs on Hulu. It’s a 1 season limited series and it’s a fantastic slow burn elevated sci fi. I loved it. Lots of the Civil War cast are in it.
3. Free State Of Jones
4. * Anniversary on Hulu or prime
* 2073 on HBO

Both ring familiar to what we’re all experiencing in the world (especially the US) today.
5. The Killing Fields (1984)
6. '71 I think has a similar feel 
7. Stratton (2017)
8. American Insurrection 
9. Leave the World Behind
10. Bushwick 

## 5. Movies with an abandoned Midwestern us vibe

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Really been into Jane remover and the whole vibe of the art for cencus Designated.

That abandoned midwest vibe with vast flat corn fields and stuff. 

Maybe it's too specific but if you look up the "cencus designated" and look at the album cover you can see what I mean maybe.

Thanks:)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Lookout

Children of the Corn

Signs

Drop Dead Gorgeous
2. Jeepers Creepers & Jeepers Creepers 2
3. The Reflecting Skin (1990)
4. First thing that propped into my head was Days of Heaven
5. A quiet place (2018)
6. Paper Moon (1973) with Ryan O'Neal and Tatum O'Neal, set in Kansas in the 1930s. Shot in black and white, to give that feeling of desolate flat midwestern lands.
7. If you move west and south, here are 2 movies with an abandoned desert vibe:

Bagdad Cafe (1987) Mohave desert, California 

How the Garcia Girls Spent their Summer (2005) southern Arizona 
8. Tideland
9. The Straight Story 

The opening scene of Hoosiers 
10. Maybe eddington?

Maybe fallout TV show. 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Favorite movie scene where a character accepts their inevitable end and embraces it. My pick is Cutler Beckett accepting his fate in Pirates of the Caribbean: At World’s End.

**Score:** 4990

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|sZRjUjcBFit6U)


  - I was going to say titanic too but the scene where the old man and woman are laying in bed together cuddling as the water fills their room. 
2. ![gif](giphy|3oKIPvND7gEInk98Eo)

Jurassic Park hunter dude. You have to appreciate the respect from hunter to hunter when he realizes he lost, and this simple line turned iconic.
3. ![gif](giphy|aQlVLkxkBg0Tu)
4. Vasquez and Gorman in Aliens

https://preview.redd.it/bawv8qixsbzg1.jpeg?width=424&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=597803230e6122f68038c4e3ebc0d99e081c56fe
5. ![gif](giphy|A5tGm61RrBHpu)
  - “They took the little ones.”
6. Jyn and Cassian in Rogue One  
![gif](giphy|g0gGanmwI0CI3mNmti)
7. ![gif](giphy|Lx4pmGXrGNGZq)
8. T2 did it well twice

https://preview.redd.it/qh83m6sm4czg1.jpeg?width=3840&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=309243d6001b6e093817b9f77c10cd8bea1db910

## 2. Apocalypto is amazing

**Score:** 2215
**Body:** I first watched this film sometime in the 2000s shortly after it came out without knowing anything about it.  Remembered enjoying it a lot and it being very action filled but that was all I recalled.

Watched it with my kids last night (they love thrillers/horror type stuff) and man was quite impressed again.  Especially when I looked it up and saw it only cost $40 Million to make.  All of the acting is absolutely top notch.  And there's so many people involved.  Something like 700 extras and 250 hair and makeup people creating amazingly intricate designs on almost all of them.  The world-building aspect for such a low budget film seems unmatched to anything I can think of post 2000.

And the action just NEVER stops.  Love it.  More movies like this please.  Anyone have similar recommendations which I may have missed?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Say what you want about Mel Gibson, but the son of a bitch knows story structure

Edit to add: this is a South Park quote, y’all
  - Ahhh my nipples hurt when you twist them!!!
2. Definitely one of the best examples of maintaining the momentum of action and peril. My favorite thing about this movie is how the mother in and son don’t get along in everyday life. But, later during their capture they display love for each other. I forget the scene though it was very well done.
  - They just sort of made eyes at each other after she was let go and he was unknowingly being led away towards the stairs of doom.  I wasn't exactly sure how to interpret that look; but I think that was again great acting.  To me it appeared like a combination of fear, respect, and empathy.
    - The mother-in-law, who had badgered him for grandchildren. Suddenly she wasn't even auctioned off as a slave, and left a stranger alone with nobody, as her only family was led away. From a matriarch awaiting grandchildren to an unwanted drifter. Incredibly sad moment.
3. I only saw it last year and I had a huge regret - that I waited 20 years to watch it. Really remarkable film in every way. Phenomenal character building.


"I am Jaguar Paw! Son of Flint Sky! This is my forest! My father hunted this forest before me! My sons and their sons will hunt here after I am gone!"
4. Yeah, and there is nothing else like it. 
  - Cause pre-Columbus America is widely ignored, or made believe the natives relationships between different tribes were a Teletubbies episode
5. Very powerful movie. Capped by them seeing the sailing ship at the end..
6. A truly unforgettable movie and I don’t say that lightly 

## 3. Movies with a "cold open" that are totally badass

**Score:** 1536

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Matrix just instantly throws you in the shit and it’s brilliant cause you’re just as confused and mixed up as the cops on the scene. 
  - https://i.redd.it/1d639ww2gezg1.gif
2. X-Men 2. Nightcrawler running through the white house was insane


  - Do date, the best sequence in an xmen movie. 

Maybe that Quicksilver sequence but it didn’t have the “oh shit, oh shit, OH SHIT” feeling I got from Nightcrawler. 
3. Lots of James Bond movies. I’m partial to Tomorrow Never Dies
4. Raiders of the Lost Ark

![gif](giphy|JOxC7fApXUki4)
5. Not a great film when compared with the original but 28 Weeks Later’s cold open is fucking phenomenal and everything I want from a fast zombie movie. Nail biting doesn’t cover it.  
  - The opening of the "Dawn of the Dead" remake was pretty good too. Sarah Polley was great in that. And then the opening credits with the devolving news footage montage over Johnny Cash's "When the man comes around". Really well done. 
6. The Dark Knight
7. Dredd (2012) with Karl Urban. His deadpan monologue about Mega City 1 leading into a police chase turned shootout is amazing to me.

## 4. One of the most underrated female actress. NAOMI HARRIS. She had one of the best performances in her role in Pirates Of The Caribbean and Black & Blue.

**Score:** 1259

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 28 days later, loved her in that. 
  - I was shocked she didn’t get more after this and the pirates movies at least akin to Thandie Newton after interview with a vampire and MI2, I wonder if it was due to the makeup of the movie meaning being less recognizable.
    - I honestly didn’t know she was the same person until this post lol, and I love 28 years so that’s possible. I think she won a small breakout award for her role in 28 years as well making it more surprising
2. Her Moneypenny in Spectre had more chemistry with Daniel Craig in their two minutes together than Léa Seydoux who was the main Bond Girl.
  - Don’t forget *Skyfall*. I’d let her shave me with a straight razor.
  - I never realized Moneypenny and Calypso were played by the same person. 
3. She was excellent in 28 Days Later. 
4. In Black and Blue she single handedly transformed below average Netflix affair in quite possibly one of the best originals they ever made.

She was that good.
5. She was unbelievably good in *Moonlight*
6. I was shocked realizing Miss Moneypenny and Calypso were portrayed by the same person! She’s awesome. 

## 5. Christopher Nolan has never made a bad movie.

**Score:** 1155
**Body:** The greatest of all time.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Why is the RT for the prestige so low!?
  - IMO its one of his best
    - IMO it’s one of THE best. Not trying to one up you as much as it sounds like it
      - IMO it’s IS THE best. Just trying to one up you.
    - His best. I have made sure every single family member of mine watches it.
  - Prestige and Interstellar are both pretty shocking. I'd say The Prestige is his best film. 
2. The Odyssey PR team is ramping up lol
3. Yeah true.  But one horrible scene that will forever be one of the biggest bad acting shots ever!

![gif](giphy|XLj32cR4MdpOE)
4. Prestige and Interstellar being in the 70s is a crime.
5. It’s now a given RT ratings can be and are gamed

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. La Femme Nikita vs Point of no Return

**Score:** 19
**Body:** First of all, watching a remake before the original is a mistake. I saw Point of No Return first, and while I truly admired Bridget Fonda, returning to La Femme Nikita was a real eye opener for how that atmosphere actually works. 

La Femme Nikita is a raw, French arthouse thriller. The setting feels authentic, the shootouts are chaotic, the missions are messy, and the killer is hesitant. She's too wild and free to be caged. Anne Parillaud delivers a standout performance. What truly makes the film breathe is Luc Besson’s masterful use of silence. It’s a visually striking, atmospheric masterpiece. 

But honestly, my instincts were thrown off by the script itself. Her severe drug addiction and withdrawal symptoms just disappear, they don't even mention it again. The agency's logic is flawed too, because no government would risk two years and millions to turn an unstable street addict into a top agent. 

The third act is awkward and inconsistent. The climax relies on Dues Ex Machina. Nikita dramatically changes her look near the end of the film and her fiancé of six months doesn't blink at the continuity slip, and the script really strips Nikita of her agency.

Now, looking at the American remake, it’s a different story. We sadly lost the intense psychological claustrophobia of the original in there.

Point of No Return replaces the gritty feel with glossy visuals, forced seduction, and heavy gunfire. Even Hans Zimmer's powerful score felt out of place, drowning out the quiet tension that made the original so gripping. Maggie’s character shifts into a typical femme fatale in the third act, betraying the vulnerability Bridget Fonda so brilliantly conveyed. 

Fonda was phenomenal though. She deserves her flowers. Her fierce disobedience and raw terror rivaled Parillaud's, sometimes even surpassing her. She managed to elevate what was otherwise a mediocre script.

And I appreciate one thing about the American version. The writers tightened up some of the loose storytelling. They fixed the disguise continuity errors and, more importantly, gave Maggie the agency to refuse her final mission, which was the standout improvement for me.

But those small fixes can’t erase the fact that Hollywood basically sanded off all the rough edges of a chaotic, feral thriller. 

In the end, we’re left with two flawed films:

Besson's brilliant atmosphere built on a shaky screenplay, vs a Hollywood machine that’s technically polished but lacking soul.

What do you guys think?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. i think you're right that both films are doing something interesting and neither one fully lands, but if you want that raw-psychological-transformation-in-a-prison vibe that the first half of La Femme Nikita nails, A Prophet (2009, Jacques Audiard) is the real successor — Algerian kid gets locked up, spends two hours becoming someone completely different through violence and survival, the training montage is just him learning to navigate the prison hierarchy, very claustrophobic and patient. The Lives of Others (2006, Florian Henckel von Donnersmarck) if you want that surveillance-state control angle, Stasi agent assigned to monitor a playwright in East Berlin and the whole thing's about institutional manipulation rewiring someone's moral compass, extremely tense without any gunfights. and for something more recent that sits between glossy Hollywood and raw European arthouse, The Guilty (2018, Gustav Möller, the Danish one not the Gyllenhaal remake) — single-location thriller, disgraced cop working 911 dispatch trying to save a kidnapped woman over the phone, whole film's just his face and voice and what you imagine is happening, very lean script with no fat on it
  - Well in your list, I have already watched The Lives Of Others and nothing, except Citizen Kane, could replace that movie for me.
2. I definitely watched this a few times as a 10 year old and remember loving it. I might check out La Femme Nikita first and watch it back to back.

I'm one of those weirdos who kinda enjoys the Vanishing remake as a weird (inferior of course) curiosity.
3. It's fun to compare the two. I like that we see some more of her actual missions in Point of No Return. Like, in Femme Nikita, IIRC, she drops off a package and is told to go home. She's all like "Huh. I thought I was a spy or assassin or something. How anticlimactic." but in the American version, she's like "Huh. That was anticlimact-" and then the entire floor of the hotel blows up and she's like "OH SHIT." or something like that. I preferred Jean Reno's 'nettoyeur' to Harvey Keitel's but they both had things to offer. In a way, they fill in each other's blanks for but different audiences. If there was a way to combine them, I think you'd get a pretty cool movie that's better than either of them.
  - You're right. Each one had its own style and substance. If we combine them for a third movie, that'll surely be a masterpiece. A French arthouse with an American style.
4. A couple of points IMHO very importantly noticed, apart from many other aspects and the inherent problems of transplanting a remake into a totally different cultural/social millieu.

1. The visuals - the remake is indeed very glossy, very competent, very self-conscious. The original had astounding feel and much edgier, one could really see (no pun intended) Besson's thinking (in the same way as e.g. Spielberg in Duel can be seen second by second thinking with the camera).
2. The music. As competent Zimmer's score is, Eric Serra's phenomenal ability to move across many moods over vast range BUT keep a recognisable consistency, plus his sonic adventures make the original's score quite remarkable even without the images. Try listen to the soundtrack album... it really works and has weight and that same edgy, gritty atmosphere that some of his other OSTs have (like parts of Subway or Le Grand Bleu).
5. I always thought it was an odd choice that for her funky lair that the remake had her rent an entire house on the Venice beach boardwalk while the original had her finding a ruined garret in Paris.
  - Yes, and apart from that, the remake showed Maggie and her bf too intimate, while the original used subtext to let us fill in the blanks.
6. Nikita is probably my favorite action movie. The twist ending the first scene is bad ass. 

How the government coerces her into the spy game is great. “What if I don’t want to? Row 8 Plot 30.

Her mission on her honeymoon is one of my favorite scenes in movie history with her husband pleading with her on the other side of the bathroom door. 

Using Jeanne Moreau, from Louis Malle’s Elevator to the Gallows, as the one to teach Nikita to use her feminine charms is a great not to the French New Wave. That she doesn’t use her feminine charms in her missions is a cool directorial choice. 

The end loses a little steam, but I think it serves the plot point that she really isn’t cut out for this sort of thing. And it allows the introduction of Jean Reno as The Cleaner which is enjoyable. 

I did see the French version first. Usually, you will stick with the first one you saw as the favorite, assuming you liked it. The American version seemed flat to me on the drama from each scene I loved in the French version. 
7. I always find remakes fascinating from a filmmaking standpoint. Watching them back-to-back can really highlight the individual choices that shape the movie, and provide insight into the personalities that make them.

For example, I had seen and enjoyed Christopher Nolan's *Insomnia* when it came out, but never knew it was a remake until much later when I was looking through Stellan Skarsgard's filmography one day. I watched the original and loved it, and then I decided to re-watch Nolan's version. I almost wish I hadn't, because all I could see were all the subtle changes Nolan had made, and none of them were improvements in my opinion. Now I can't watch any Nolan movie without seeing all his quirks and wondering what the "better" version of the story would look like.

## 2. Films that felt close to a musical peice.

**Score:** 11
**Body:** Films that felt close to a musical peice you liked.

I still remember the first time I saw 'Paris, Texas'. The films starts and pulls you right into it depriving you of any context or information. Yet you feel emotional and sad. And I have always felt the same about 'Shine On You Crazy Diamond' by Pink Floyd. We dont hear the first words until its halfway through the song. Yet you start to feel something. 

Michael Mann's 'Miami Vice' really felt like a modern orchestration of electronic and pop pieces .

I would like to know about any piece of music and films you find closely related, in its essence, like the ones I mentioned above. 

Feel free to expand the discussion on the nature of music or film and its relative qualities.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *Dead Man*. It features of the most immersive sound-related cinematic experiences of modern motion pictures. Few films have excelled at creating such a fascinating atmosphere, which feels so smooth and organic at the same time.

From the get go, *Dead Man*'s all-encompassing music feels like some mesmerizing sonic mantra. Neil Young's subtle dronings enhance the timeless landscape of the movie. You're almost *watching* sounds.

While the obvious comment would be something like 'the music becomes just another character,' I think that *Dead Man*'s soundtrack performs as some spiritual entity, oscillating between the mysterious sounds of nature and the immortal souls of Native American people.
2. Not sure if this answer fits, but Sirat comes to mind. The music is so important/key to the characters and setting. It’s mostly instrumental and there’s certainly no characters singing, but I can’t stop thinking about how the music is integrated into the movie & story. 
  - Sirat makes sense. The music is so much ingrained into the film. But I dont think the film itself feels like a musical piece, atleast for me though.  
3. I've always thought that the fractured, surreal qualities of Has' *The Hourglass Sanatorium* were reminiscent of Mahler's Seventh Symphony, especially the first Nachtmusik. It's a very strange piece of music, one which exchanges major and minor modes so extensively that you never really know where you are. Sometimes they occur simultaneously, which generates some jarring dissonances. The dreamlike shifts in the film -- ideas being introduced and then vanishing at the drop of a hat -- feels to me like the cinematic equivalent of that modal mixture. It can be quite disorienting. 

On the surface level you also have those "old Europe" topics. In Mahler's case, use of melodies reminiscent of Austro-Hungarian marching tunes, and in Has' case the film is obviously in part a trip through the late 19th and early 20th centuries in Austria-Hungary. IIRC Franz Joseph is even in the film as one of the wax figurines.
4. Interesting topic, this actually made me stop and think about how I tend to experience certain films more like pieces of music than narratives.

I’m with you on *Paris, Texas* and *Miami Vice*. *Paris, Texas* especially has that quality where it just starts and you’re already inside a feeling before you understand anything. It’s doing what something like “Shine On You Crazy Diamond” does—holding back any kind of clear statement, but still establishing tone immediately. You’re already emotionally oriented before the “meaning” arrives.

A few others came to mind for similar reasons, though they all get there in slightly different ways.

*Chungking Express* feels almost ambient in structure. It’s not really building toward anything in a conventional sense. It loops, drifts, repeats. The emotional weight comes from rhythm and timing more than plot. It reminds me of GAS or Brian Eno's Music for Airports that kind of hypnotic, cyclical music where you’re just sitting inside a texture and small changes start to matter more than big events.

*Burning* feels closer to slowcore or shoegaze. There’s a lot of space, a lot of stillness, and whatever meaning is there feels buried rather than expressed. It accumulates instead of progressing. By the end, it hits, but not because anything was spelled out more because of what’s been quietly building the entire time.

*In the Mood for Love* is almost minimalist composition. It’s repetition with variation. Same corridors, same movements, the same musical phrases returning. But each time, something shifts slightly. It deepens instead of advancing, which is closer to how a piece of music works than a traditional narrative arc.

*Drive* is more obvious, but it still fits. It plays like a long synth-pop track, very clean, very controlled, built around strong motifs and tone. Dialogue almost feels secondary to mood, like everything is being carried by texture and pacing instead.

*Mandy* is basically a doom metal album. It’s slow, heavy, saturated everything feels distorted, and kinetic. It’s less about what happens and more about being submerged in a sustained state.

*Portrait of a Lady on Fire* feels closer to chamber music of someone like Arvo Pärt. Extremley minimal, slow and sparse. Comes off almost more devotional and  is extremely restrained and precise, but that restraint is what gives the few moments of release so much weight. When it opens up, it really opens up.

*The Virgin Suicides* sits in that dream-pop / shoegaze space pretty naturally Slowdive Soulvaki album immediately comes to mind esp songs like When the Sun Hits. It’s hazy, nostalgic, and more about atmosphere than causality. It doesn’t feel like it’s trying to explain anything, just preserve a feeling.

*Blue Valentine* feels raw in a different way more like Velvet Underground or Sonic Youth. It’s intimate but unpolished, almost abrasive at times. Emotionally it feels “unmixed,” like you’re getting it without any smoothing which is alot like the film. Very raw and unfiltered almost documentary style filmmakiing. 

*Lost Highway* is probably the closest thing to industrial music structurally. It’s fragmented, disorienting, constantly shifting identity. The fact that Trent Reznor is involved makes sense, but even beyond the soundtrack, the film itself behaves like that kind of composition.

And then something like Mallicks *Days of Heaven* or *Picnic at Hanging Rock* feel almost like early ambient pieces in film form. They’re less concerned with narrative causality and more with mood, light, and drift. They don’t really move forward so much as they hover. This can also be heavily applied to Mallicks later films Tree of Life, Knight of Cups etc...

I think what ties all of these together is that they prioritize tone and rhythm over plot. They don’t really “tell” you things in a direct way as much as they let emotion build the way music does. You’re not following them so much as sitting inside them and letting them work on you.

## 3. Interpretations of the baby in Eraserhead (David Lynch, 1977) and why it might represent emotional responsibility made physical.

**Score:** 9
**Body:** One of the most unsettling elements in *Eraserhead* is the so-called “baby,” which David Lynch never explained directly. Rather than treating it as a literal child or a simple symbol of fatherhood anxiety, I think it works better as something more abstract: emotional responsibility turned into physical form.

In this reading, the baby is not just something Henry has to care for, but something that exists because of him, a manifestation of guilt, fear, and the burden of connection itself. Its constant suffering and deformity reflect not only horror, but the idea that emotional obligations can become inescapable and almost biological in weight.

I am curious how others approach it. Do you see the baby primarily as psychological symbolism, surrealist body horror, or something more metaphysical about existence and responsibility?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In my interpretation, the baby is deformed as a lens through which Henry sees his situation, and his guilt. In my interpretation, the film takes place purely in a dream, and the baby may or may not be born yet (“It’s premature but it’s a baby!”) Henry is fundamentally unable to see himself as a suitable father, both because he is financially struggling, and because of his tendency towards infidelity. It seems that Henry doesn’t have much control over his sexual urges, and likely had an affair with his neighbor. Now, a father and provider for his family, must house the mother of his child only feet away from his partner in infidelity. As Henry sees it, this situation can only end horribly for him, and Henry aims his despair over this situation into the baby, who he sees as disfigured and demonic, an entity which he sees as the sole cause of his problems, when his own actions as well as the conditions of society led him to this situation. In the end, I believe Henry opts for suicide as his only option.

I love this film so much. Ive seen it at least a dozen times. It’s what got me into taking cinema more seriously. It will always hold a special place in my heart.
2. The way he continues trying to console the baby, even after he's run out of ideas pretty quickly and is just sort of grimacing along with it, is significant to me. As if suffering alongside the child and bearing witness to its pain is somehow equally important to actually, y'know, helping in any way.

It's like he sees parentage as this constant obligation to 'show up' that has almost no logic or baseline of competency.
3. I see it as a real baby and the way it appears is a metaphorical look at how worried he is about fatherhood. Most things Lynch does are simple like this. They are not as convoluted as the fanbase thinks.
  - I think it's a fairly safe assumption that Lynch made the film as a result of anxiety around fatherhood, but I do respect that he is always so reluctant to explain these things directly. For me, I think with Eraserhead you can extrapolate and take the meaning to be not just fatherhood but of burden in general. That burden can be down to responsibility, but it can also be something else like guilt, trauma, or anxiety. Something that prevents you from acting on things. I think the film ends up being a lot more universal as a result.
  - his daughter was also born with clubbed feet iirc or some other mild physical deformity that he clearly felt somewhat responsible for. The eraserhead baby's form itself clearly invokes the shape of a clubbed limb as well, I've always thought, and his work is concerned with the separation of limbs as individual identities.
  - The actual experience of fatherhood can intersect with desire/repulsion and identity/negation in all sorts of ways. The limited range of comfortable ways for men to engage and process these experiences can add to a sense of isolation and further negation. The workplace is potentially an outlet for this, but challenging in its own ways. 
For me Lynch presented this at pretty much face value, if you can connect emotionally with the experiences he brought to it. 
Nothing wrong with reading it your own way, though. 
4. for me it’s vibe. the vibe is he thinks he is ready, but he isn’t, and he can’t get himself any help. that could be applied to parenthood, relationships, the self, whatever. it’s an intentionally open symbol. they’re not even sure it is a baby.  
they all get wrapped up in one another, but at the end of the day, the baby is pretty ready to give you the feeling of that disconnected anxiety rather than directly represent it

## 4. What makes a character hard to connect with in a film?

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I've heard of some directors being criticized for being cold, with characters that are hard to care for. 

I saw some criticism of this kind for the three-hour long movie Oppenheimer. 

Someone who only shows up for a scene or is an extra is one thing, but what makes a character difficult to connect to for you, even in a film where they have lots of screentime and no shortage of scenes and dialogue?
I don't want this discussion to focus on any one specific director or film like Oppenheimer, but the bigger subject.
  
While this subject can be subjective, what's your opinion and feeling on this anyway?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I often feel this way about Chris Nolan films, to a lesser degree with Kubrick, and an even greater degree with Zach Snyder. Their characters, to me, don’t display much humanity or weakness, and it lends to them feeling robotic. Not in an acting performance way, but in an “unnaturally perfect and effective” way. Their goals are so lofty that when they succeed, it feels unearned in a way. And they regularly don’t get across the trauma and exertion to accomplishing their goals. 

I didn’t feel this way about Oppenheimer, because it’s biopic nature, the man really did live a life grander than what most people would consider human. Kubrick works for me despite this feeling, because to me his characters feel less integral to the stories he tells, and they act more like archetypes to be moved through the story than as people to me. Zach Snyder tbh doesn’t belong in conversation with these other two filmmakers, although I do feel his character writing suffers similarly to them.
2. Kubrick makes me smile, because he tends to push his audience.  In several of his films, he'll show the warts of his characters early on (Alex, Humbert, Barry) and then put them in a situation where your question, as a viewer, is "do I sympathize with them or not?"

There's nothing wrong with \*not\* sympathizing; but, by that same token, there's nothing "wrong" with refusing to feel sympathy for the sniper in FMJ  (there, too, Kubrick shows a sniper that withholds the benefit of a quick death, and then, after her reversal, asks "can you now muster sympathy?").

Lesser filmmakers might give us "permission" not to care about somebody, not to sympathize with them, because of their warts.   Kubrick doesn't; he doesn't tell how you \*must\* feel (e.g., "I don't care, I side with Bullingdon over Barry"; "I side with Animal Mother regarding the sniper"), but you're responsible for your own reaction.

Kubrick baits our reactions in a similar way to the baiting of Alex in "Clockwork":  "The boy you've always quarreled with is seriously ill."
  - Yes, I don't need to "connect" with any character necessarily, as long as I can connect to the filmmaking as a whole; mood, tone, story, visual approach, the themes it lays out etc. Kubrick is a prime example, although I find him less cold than some people, he is very subtle in giving the traditional emotional beats to a character, but they can be found (in FMJ for example, the way Joker covers his ears to not hear Pyle cry in agony, or the way he looks at the wounded sniper)
  - I think it's telling the most explicitly pathetic scene in all of Kubrick is HAL's 'death scene' when he's being disconnected. "Dave... My mind is going... I can feel it.."
3. Lack of close-ups.

You might think I am kidding.  I am not.  The secret to people connecting with a character is to give that character a lot of close-ups and do not give other characters much if any.  Try it, you'll see.

On my last film an actor complained to me that I kept referring to her character as the main character when the other character had most of the dialogue.  I explained that she will be the main character because she's getting all the close-ups.  She thought I was nuts, until she saw the finished film.

  
This works because cinema is a visual art form.   Take any scene in movie history.  Take, I dunno, the beach scene in *Jaws*.  Don't change one word of the script but take all of Roy Scheider's close-ups away and give them to Mrs. Kitner.  Poof, Mrs. Kitner is now who your audience connects with.

This won't make you **like** a character, but it will make you connect with them.


4. I dont care if I have nothing in common with the character, I don't care if I know anyone like the character, I don't care if the character is a good person or likable. They need to feel convincing as entities (consistent, operating under certain logic and principles etc), rather than serving as an illustration of some message or a tool to deliver it. 

And more importantly, there shouldn't be an intent to make me feel a specific thing for this character that clashes with my experience while watching them.

E.g. I can enjoy an evil, psychotic or boring or selfish character who seems real, but the moment I feel that the movie/writing/direction starts asking me to see this person as good, righteous, interesting or sympathetic, Ill get annoyed and disconnect. I think that's really the issue for most people. Its not that someone's unlikable, it's that you find someone unlikable but the narrative insists that you should like them, admire them, feel for them or similar sentiments


But yeah I also dont watch to see myself or find  a role model, so people whose criticisms revolve around those points are idiots.
5. Just finished watching Antonioni’s L’Avventura, which is part of his alienation trilogy and where he did not want us to identify with the characters. He achieves it partly by refusing to give us reaction shots in dialogue – characters often speak with their backs to us, with two characters speaking one of them is completely unreadable to us because we can’t see their face, and sometimes both characters have their backs turned to us. Lots of long takes in impressive landscapes that overwhelm the characters as well– again, often with their backs to us – and added to that they don’t act very rationally, but we don’t know enough about their pasts to have any idea why they are doing what they’re doing. They are ciphers to one another and to us watching it. 

I think it’s helpful to look at someone who’s doing it on purpose because then you can see how other direction decisions can actually create the same effect. In Nolan’s case the choice to film Oppenheimer in a nonlinear way, with no attention to historicity so the characters just float without anchor, with constant cutting from one scene floating free in time to another, and with the intent that we are very aware of the director manipulating us and the characters – it creates the same kind of alienation, for a lot of the same what reason. Disconnecting the characters from their time and place leaves us either working very hard to put together cause and effect for them, or deprives us of the ability to do it at all. Once we lose continuity on a character, it’s hard to care because we can’t identify with them except at the most basic level.

And the constant awareness that Nolan’s hand lays heavily on everything you’re seeing adds an incredible layer of distance. I’m not as caught up in the character if I’m thinking about the director, or if I find the character false, as was often the case in that film. I don’t think I wasn’t as aware of Antonioni actually, watching L’Avventura, he managed his manipulation so seamlessly, but then Nolan’s Oppenheimer is at hardly at the same level as Antonioni so that might be it.
6. For me, it's when the character does not behave in a way that is realistic to the scenario. To be sure, the situation can be absurd, that's not a problem at all, hell the character can be a complete psychopath, but they must act in a way that a psychopath would act.

Dimwitted high schoolers, for example, can have some depth - stupidity and immaturity do not negate real feelings - but they can't put on a pair of glasses and start doing advanced calculus.
7. It's a very nebulous thing but my theory is that it's not about characters, it's about **relationships**. It's about what one character thinks of another, and how they interact, and the emotions they instill in each other, and how that relationship changes each character. The mistake filmmakers make is that it's NOT about the audience connecting - but about the characters in the story connecting. That's what the audience responds to. 

One of the reasons we watch movies is to understand human relationships, and we respond warmly when they're well depicted on screen. So we connect to characters who are able to connect with each other, whether it's love or hate or respect or disgust. You can make a great hero character, or a flawed hero character, but the audience won't connect with them in any way unless they have a real relationship with another character in the story. It's why Superman has Lois Lane. Their relationship is what makes the characters relatable and human, not his super powers or the action or his perfect looks.

I know you don't want to pick on Oppenheimer, but Nolan's a great example of a filmmaker who can't do relationships. Part of Oppenheimer's problem is that it's non-linear. He's already having an affair in the story before the scenes where he meets his wife. How do you connect with that relationship? But then you have films like Dunkirk, where there are no relationships whatsoever, and Interstellar, where the relationships are maudlin and ham-fisted. He's just not good at it, so he relies on his gimmicks instead.
8. Oftentimes I think that a character who is hard connect with is due to the script failing to place him in situations that might make the viewer think to themselves, “oh, yeah, I’ve definitely been \*there\*.”  
As an example, there’s A Fish Called Wanda - right away in the opening scenes all these characters feel real and relatable because the script does a fantastic job of creating a world that resembles the world the audience actually lives in on a daily basis. Surely there are other factors, but a good script can be the difference here.

## 5. difference between dubbing and lip sync for movies?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** &#x200B;

So I didnt fully knew this but the reason dubbed content feels off isnt usually bad voice acting.

different languages have different rhythms, syllable counts, mouth shapes. Spanish and Hindi are syllable timed that means every syllable gets roughly equal time whereas english is stress-timed with syllables stretch etc.

So when you translate and record, the mouth movements dont match the new audio, and so it feels off

Traditional dubbing "solves" this by rewriting the script to fit mouth shapes which often changes meaning. And new AI lip sync tools like 11 Labs or Sync Labs are approaching it differently they reanimate the lip movements to match the new audio instead of forcing the audio to match old lip movements

That's an insanely different approach. But what do you think how far will this work and if it would scale as well?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'll never understand why people downgrade the viewing experience by choosing dubbing over subtitles. You're missing so much of the experience if you don't hear the actual voices of the actors. 
  - 100% agree, a great part of acting is about voice and speech.. watching dubbed actors when you know their voice is really painful
  - Funny anecdote: for many years, I had never understood why people were making fun of Keanu Reeves in *Dracula*. This was back during the VHS days. In my naive and unformed mind, he was a decent actor, doing a fine job—until I watched the undubbed version. What I want to say is that there are rare cases where a dubbed version actually enhances the viewing experience.
  - Yeah, I fully switched to subtitled. I guess that makes me a cinephile here. Often called snobby by others xD
  - Sadly there are a lot of people who legitimately can’t read fast enough to keep up with subtitles.

Edit: what a strange thing to downvote me for
2. It's not just that,

coming from a country where everything is dubbed - Germany:

There is just much less immersion for multiple reasons:

\- It makes no sense that in American film set in the USA, everybody is talking German, for example. Especially with famous actors. You know that they can't speak German

\- There are only a few dozen regular voice actors and almost everyone has a distinctive "radio voice", while many actors have regular sounding voices. 

\- They are speaking in a non natural way  (I heard other languages have this too, spanish for example)

\- Studio sound recording becomes obvious, the sound is too clear at all times

\- Lower budget films sometimes have poor dubbing, called "porn dub" here

  
In Germany, they are quite good with lip-syncing, you only notice this if you look really close at mouth movements. In films where only a few actors are dubbed (because they have a strong accent for example) it's often hard to pinpoint who is dubbed.


  - Regarding Spanish: there is a Standard Spanish dubbing system, where geographically-neutral words —which most Spanish speakers will understand completely— are used. That system (in my opinion) produces the weirdest, fakest-sounding dubbing because, as you mentioned, nobody speaks like that. It is widely used in TV channels like HBO, Discovery Channels, etc. But there is a Latin Spanish version (perhaps done mostly by Mexican dubbing actors) which flows in a more natural way to my ears. Some U. S.-produced shows have risen to prominence in México because of witty dubbing (Top Cat was a cartoon show which kind of flopped in the U. S., but it became a staple show in Mexico during the eighties because it took many liberties with dialogue in order to make it funnier). Many Latin American countries get their dubbing from Mexico; I don't know what their listening experience is like.
3. Have you watched Polish TV channels? When they show a film which is not Polish, they do the strangest thing (to me, at least). They play the film in its original language. And once you think "Ok, this is in English, I will get it), a Polish dub in higher volumen crashes the party, so you get to hear both languages superimposed.


But the weirdest part: the SAME person dubs ALL characters. And the emotion in speech Is normally just lacking completely.


With all respect to the Polish people, but this Is the weirdest dubbing system I have seen so far.
  - Having downloaded movies from a certain russian site they do the same thing, but I just assumed it was because it was pirated stuff. Seems crazy to do for actual releases.
4. >rewriting the script to fit mouth shapes which often changes meaning.

this doesn't happen, or at least it's not the norm. They might change the way it's *expressed*, making it a non literal translation, but they're (generally speaking) not going to change the meaning just to fit the lip movements, they're more likely to sacrifice a bit of the sync than to change the line to something entirely different

(They might change a line for different reasons, tho, some better than others. In all translation there's a degree of adaptation)
